chain_of_thought: Top native frame is ks_memcpy in libCertJavaClient.so; decompilation shows it is just an indirect jump via PTR_ks_memcpy_001804e8, so actual memcpy implementation is elsewhere (likely in another lib or internal blob).

Next frame down is _dPKIHeader, also just an indirect call via PTR__dPKIHeader_00180958. There is no direct C code here; real logic lives in the function pointer targets which are not resolved in this view, so I cannot inspect the concrete memcpy arguments or buffers.

However, the library map and xrefs show ks_memcpy is used heavily in ASN.1/CMP-related helpers (e.g., __OCTETSTRING, _PKIHeader, __EncryptedVID etc.)—typical encoding/decoding utilities that copy variable-length data. These usually take lengths derived from input or protocol fields, not compile‑time constants, so misuse could yield OOB reads/writes.

The crash termination is at ks_memcpy, strongly indicating a memory-safety issue (e.g., buffer overflow, invalid pointer) rather than an intentional abort. Allocator/sanitizer frames are not shown, just ks_memcpy itself, consistent with a hard fault in memcpy or a wrapper around it.

Backing up the call chain: JNI bridge Java_com_koscom_cert_ca_CANative_cmpIssue2 gets two Java byte arrays (param_3 and param_4) and two ints, uses JNI GetByteArrayElements-like callbacks (offset 0x5c0) to get raw pointers uVar2/uVar3, and then calls CMP_Certificate_2(uVar2,uVar3,param_5,param_6). CMP_Certificate_2 is not decompiled here, but by name and context it is a high-level operation that will end up building/decoding PKI structures via _PKIHeader/_dPKIHeader and, through those, ks_memcpy.

Critically, the ints param_5 and param_6 (Java int arguments) are passed straight into CMP_Certificate_2 with no validation or bounds checks in the JNI bridge. Those integers are attacker-controlled from Java caller (and in fuzzing, directly from fuzzer), and will likely be used as lengths or flags in lower-level routines that eventually call ks_memcpy. If a length is larger than the actual buffer or negative/overflowed, ks_memcpy would read/write out of bounds and crash.

Because I cannot decompile CMP_Certificate_2 or the actual implementation behind PTR__dPKIHeader_00180958 / PTR_ks_memcpy_001804e8, the exact arithmetic is unknown, but the pattern (JNI passes unvalidated lengths to a complex crypto/protocol stack that frequently uses a custom memcpy wrapper and crashes inside it) is very typical of real memory-corruption vulnerabilities.

This is not a harness-only artifact: in production, Java code could invoke CANative.cmpIssue2 with attacker-influenced arguments (e.g., remote data used as cert blobs and lengths). Even if typical app UI constrains them, a malicious app or modified client could call the JNI directly. The vulnerable surface is therefore realistically reachable by untrusted input.

Given the strong evidence of memcpy-based crash, attacker-controlled ints flowing unvalidated into native protocol code, and lack of contrary evidence (like fixed-size, fully validated len values), I judge this as a likely memory-corruption vulnerability, though not fully confirmed because key implementations are hidden behind function pointers.

Severity is high: if ks_memcpy is writing out of bounds on heap/stack under controllable parameters, this can plausibly lead to code execution or at least serious memory corruption; at minimum, it is a reliable native DoS.
reasons: Crash occurs inside ks_memcpy, strongly suggesting invalid pointer or size leading to memory corruption.
JNI bridge Java_com_koscom_cert_ca_CANative_cmpIssue2 forwards attacker-controlled int parameters directly to CMP_Certificate_2 without validation.
PKI/CMP helper stack heavily uses ks_memcpy for variable-length ASN.1 structures, making incorrect length handling very plausible and exploitable.
Actual memcpy wrapper and _dPKIHeader implementations are via unresolved function pointers, so detailed bounds logic cannot be confirmed, but the pattern matches typical vulnerable designs.
recommendations: Decompile or otherwise resolve the implementations behind PTR__dPKIHeader_00180958 and PTR_ks_memcpy_001804e8, and specifically audit CMP_Certificate_2 for all uses of ks_memcpy, ensuring explicit bounds checks on all length parameters derived from Java inputs.
Add strict validation in Java_com_koscom_cert_ca_CANative_cmpIssue2 for param_5/param_6 (e.g., non-negative, within actual buffer lengths of the byte arrays) before calling CMP_Certificate_2.
Instrument builds with ASan/UBSan and targeted tests around cmpIssue2 and CMP_Certificate_2, mutating input lengths and cert blobs to reproduce and localize the exact failing memcpy call.
assumptions: CMP_Certificate_2 is the main consumer of the JNI-provided buffers and int parameters and eventually builds/decodes PKI headers using _PKIHeader/_dPKIHeader.
param_5 and/or param_6 are used as size/length or count values in lower-level routines that call ks_memcpy.
The crash reported as ks_memcpy is due to actual memory access violation rather than a manual guard/assert in ks_memcpy.
limitations: Implementations behind PTR__dPKIHeader_00180958, PTR_ks_memcpy_001804e8, and CMP_Certificate_2 were not visible, so the exact faulty memcpy call site is inferred but not proven.
Stack trace did not show allocator/sanitizer context, so precise nature of the fault (OOB read vs write vs null deref) is unknown.
No direct evidence from production call patterns (only fuzz harness plus static analysis), so real-world reachability of extreme parameter values is inferred from typical JNI exposure patterns.
